name: py532lib

metadata.name: py532lib
metadata.summary: Python wrapper around C library for RFID 532 reader

documentation:
py532lib
========

This package contains the useful stuff to communicate with a PN532 NFC chip in Python.

@author:  DanyO <me@danyo.ca>
@license: The source code within this file is licensed under the BSD 2 Clause license.
          See LICENSE file for more information.

Built for Python3, using Quick2Wire's Python APIs for the RaspberryPI.

Losely inspired from Adafruit's PN532 I2C library.

Pull requests are welcomed.

Low-level register access and a high-level application-programming
interface for the MCP23017 I2C GPIO expander.

Low level access to the MCP23017 registers

The MCP23017 has two register addressing modes, depending on the
value of bit7 of IOCON. We assume bank=0 addressing (which is the
POR default value).

modules.name: quick2wire.parts.pcf8591
documentation:
API for the PCF8591 I2C A/D D/A converter.

The PCF8591 chip has four physical input pins, named AIN0 to AIN3, at
which it measures voltage, and a single physical output pin, named
AOUT, at which it generates analogue output.

Applications  control the  chip by  setting  or getting  the state  of
logical channels that the chip maps to its physical pins. There is one
output channel and  up to four input channels,  depending on the mode.
Input channels  are either _single-ended_, measuring the  voltage on a
input pin, or _differential_, measuring the voltage difference between
two input pins.

Applications talk to the chip via objects of the PCF8591 class. A
PCF8591 object is created with an I2CMaster, through which it
communicates with the chip, and a mode, one of:

FOUR_SINGLE_ENDED -- four single-ended channels reporting voltage at
                     AIN0 to AIN3, no differential inputs.

THREE_DIFFERENTIAL -- three differential inputs reporting voltage
                      difference between AIN0 to AIN2 and AIN3. No
                      single-ended channels.

SINGLE_ENDED_AND_DIFFERENTIAL -- two single ended channels reporting
                                 voltage at AIN0 and AIN1 and one
                                 differential channel reporting the
                                 voltage difference between AIN2 and
                                 AIN3.

TWO_DIFFERENTIAL -- two differential channels, the first reporting the
                    voltage difference between AIN0 and AIN1, and the
                    second reporting the voltage difference between
                    AIN2 and AIN3. No single-ended channels.

(See the documentation for the PCF8591 class for additional, optional
constructor parameters.)

For example:

    with I2CMaster() as i2c:
        adc = PCF8591(i2c, SINGLE_ENDED_AND_DIFFERENTIAL)
        
        assert adc.single_ended_input_count == 2
        assert adc.differential_input_count == 1


Once created you can use the channels of the PCF8591.  Input channels
are obtained from the `single_ended_input` and `differential_input`
methods.

    input = adc.single_ended_input(0)
    dinput = adc.differential_input(0)

The analogue signal of a channel is obtained by querying its `value`
property.  For single-ended channels the value varies between 0 and 1.
For differential channels the value varies between -0.5 and 0.5,
because the PCF8591 chip can only detect voltage differences of half
that between its reference voltage and ground.

The output channel must be opened before use, to turn on the chip's
D/A converter, and closed when no longer required, to turn it off
again and conserve power. It's easiest to do this with a context
manager.  When turned on, assigning a value between 0 and 1 to the
output channel's `value` property with set the voltage at the chip's
physical AOUT pin:

    with adc.output as output:
        # the D/A converter in the chip is now turned on
        output.value = 0.75
   
    # at the end of the with statement the D/A converter is turned off again

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.pcf8591.PCF8591.__init__
documentation:
Initialises a PCF8591.

Parameters:
master -- the I2CMaster with which to communicate with the
          PCF8591 chip.
mode -- one of FOUR_SINGLE_ENDED, TWO_DIFFERENTIAL, 
        THREE_DIFFERENTIAL or SINGLE_ENDED_AND_DIFFERENTIAL.
address -- the I2C address of the PCF8591 chip.
           (optional, default = BASE_ADDRESS)

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.pcf8591.PCF8591.differential_input
documentation: Returns the n'th differential analogue input channel

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.pcf8591.PCF8591.read_differential

API to query and control an PCF8591 A/D and D/A converter via I2C.

See module documentation for details on how to use this class.

functions.name: quick2wire.i2c.reading
documentation: An I2C I/O message that reads n_bytes bytes of data

functions.name: quick2wire.i2c.reading_into
documentation: An I2C I/O message that reads into an existing ctypes string buffer.

functions.name: quick2wire.i2c.writing_bytes
documentation:
An I2C I/O message that writes one or more bytes of data. 

Each byte is passed as an argument to this function.

functions.name: quick2wire.i2c.writing
documentation:
An I2C I/O message that writes one or more bytes of data.

The bytes are passed to this function as a sequence.

classes.name: quick2wire.i2c.I2CMaster

methods.name: quick2wire.i2c.I2CMaster.__init__
documentation:
Opens the bus device.

Arguments:
n                -- the number of the bus (default is
                    the bus on the Raspberry Pi accessible
                    via the header pins).
extra_open_flags -- extra flags passed to posix.open when 
                    opening the I2C bus device file (default 0; 
                    e.g. no extra flags).

methods.name: quick2wire.i2c.I2CMaster.close
documentation: Closes the I2C bus device.

methods.name: quick2wire.i2c.I2CMaster.transaction
documentation:
Perform an I2C I/O transaction.

Arguments:
*msgs -- I2C messages created by one of the reading, reading_into,
         writing or writing_bytes functions.

Returns: a list of byte sequences, one for each read operation 
         performed.

Performs I2C I/O transactions on an I2C bus.

Transactions are performed by passing one or more I2C I/O messages
to the transaction method of the I2CMaster.  I2C I/O messages are
created with the reading, reading_into, writing and writing_bytes
functions defined in the quick2wire.i2c module.

An I2CMaster acts as a context manager, allowing it to be used in a
with statement.  The I2CMaster's file descriptor is closed at
the end of the with statement and the instance cannot be used for
further I/O.

For example:

    from quick2wire.i2c import I2CMaster, writing
    
    with I2CMaster() as i2c:
        i2c.transaction(
            writing(0x20, bytes([0x01, 0xFF])))

modules.name: quick2wire.asm_generic_ioctl
documentation: Warning: not part of the published Quick2Wire API.
Converted from <asm-generic/ioctl.h>
ioctl command encoding: 32 bits total, command in lower 16 bits,
size of the parameter structure in the lower 14 bits of the
upper 16 bits.
Encoding the size of the parameter structure in the ioctl request
is useful for catching programs compiled with old versions
and to avoid overwriting user space outside the user buffer area.
The highest 2 bits are reserved for indicating the ``access mode''.
NOTE: This limits the max parameter size to 16kB -1 !
The following is for compatibility across the various Linux
platforms.  The generic ioctl numbering scheme doesn't really enforce
a type field.  De facto, however, the top 8 bits of the lower 16
bits are indeed used as a type field, so we might just as well make
this explicit here.  Please be sure to use the decoding macros
below from now on.

functions.name: quick2wire.asm_generic_ioctl._IO
documentation: used to create ioctl numbers

modules.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17
documentation:
Low-level register access and a high-level application-programming
interface for the MCP23x17 series of GPIO expanders.

The definitions in this module are common to the I2C MCP23017 and SPI
MCP23S17. Only the methods for reading and writing to registers
differ, and they must be defined by subclassing the Registers class.

functions.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.deferred_read
documentation: A PinBank read mode: read() must be called explicitly.
Read and write modes

functions.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.immediate_read
documentation:
A PinBank read mode: read() is called automatically whenever a pin value is read.

Note: this mode is not compatible with interrupts. A warning will
be issued if interrupts are enabled on a PinBank that is in
immediate_read mode.

functions.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.deferred_write
documentation: A PinBank write mode: write() must be called explicitly.

functions.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.immediate_write
documentation: A PinBank write mode: registers are written whenever Pin attributes are set.

variables.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.IOCONB
documentation: Actually addresses the same register as IOCONA

classes.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Registers

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Registers.reset
documentation: Reset to power-on state

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Registers.write_banked_register
documentation: Write the value of a register within a bank.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Registers.read_banked_register
documentation: Read the value of a register within a bank.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Registers.write_register
documentation:
Write the value of a register.

Implement in subclasses.

Parameters:
reg   -- the register address
value -- the new value of the register

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Registers.read_register
documentation:
Read the value of a register.

Implement in subclasses.

Parameters:
reg   -- the register address

Returns: the value of the register.

Abstract interface for reading/writing MCP23x17 registers over the I2C or SPI bus.

You shouldn't normally need to use this class.

The MCP23x17 has two register addressing modes, depending on the
value of bit7 of IOCON. We assume bank=0 addressing (which is the
POR default value).

classes.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBanks

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBanks.__len__
documentation: Returns the number of pin banks. (2 for the MCP23x17)

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBanks.bank
documentation: Returns bank n.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBanks.reset
documentation:
Resets the chip to power-on state and sets configuration flags in the IOCON register

Parameters:
interrupt_polarity   -- sets the polarity of the interrupt output 
                        pin: 1 = active-high. 0 = active-low.
interrupt_open_drain -- configures the interrupt output pin as an 
                        open-drain output.
                        True = Open-drain output (overrides the 
                        interrupt_polarity).
                        False = Active driver output (the 
                        interrupt_polarity parameter sets the 
                        polarity).
interrupt_mirror     -- Sets the interrupt output mirroring.
                        True = the interrupt output pins are 
                        internally connected.
                        False = the interrupt output pins are 
                        not connected, INTA is associated with
                        PortA and INTB is associated with PortB.
                        Should be set to True (the default) if 
                        using the Quick2Wire MCP23017 expander 
                        board.
The pin banks of an MCP23x17 chip.

classes.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBank

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBank.index
documentation: The index of this bank (0 or 1).

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBank.__len__
documentation: The number of pins in the bank. (8 for the MCP23x17)

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBank.pin
documentation: Returns pin n.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBank.read
documentation:
Read the GPIO input and interrupt capture registers from the chip.

If the bank's read_mode is set to deferred_read, this must be
called to make value property of the bank's Pins reflect the
state of the chip's physical input pins.

If the bank's read_mode is set to immediate_read, read() is
called whenever the value property of any of the bank's Pins
is read.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.PinBank.write
documentation:
Write changes to the pin's state capture and GPIO input registers from the chip.

If the bank's write_mode is set to deferred_write, this must be
update the chip's physical input pins so that they reflect the
value property of the bank's Pins.

If the bank's write_mode is set to immediate_write, write() is
called whenever the value property of any of the bank's Pins
is set.
A bank of 8 GPIO pins

classes.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Pin

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Pin.__init__
documentation: Called by the PinBank.  Not used by application code.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Pin.open
documentation:
Acquire the Pin for use.

Raises: ValueError if the pin is already in use.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Pin.get
documentation:
Returns the value of the pin.  

The same as pin.value, but a method so that it can easily be passed around as a function.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Pin.set
documentation:
Sets the value of the pin.  

The same as pin.value, but a method so that it can easily be passed around as a function.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Pin.enable_interrupts
documentation:
Signal an interrupt on the bank's interrupt line whenever the value of the pin changes.

Parameters:
value -- If set, the interrupt is signalled when the pin's value is changed to this value.
         If not set, the interrupt is signalled whenever the pin's value changes.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Pin.disable_interrupts
documentation: Do not signal an interrupt when the value of the pin changes.

methods.name: quick2wire.parts.mcp23x17.Pin.interrupt
documentation:
Has the pin signalled an interrupt that has not been services?

True:  the pin has signalled an interrupt
False: the pin has not signalled an interrupt
A digital Pin that can be used for input or output.

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_REALTIME
documentation: Identifier for system-wide realtime clock.

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_MONOTONIC
documentation: Monotonic system-wide clock.

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID
documentation: High-resolution timer from the CPU

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_THREAD_CPUTIME_ID
documentation: Thread-specific CPU-time clock.

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW
documentation: Monotonic system-wide clock, not adjusted for frequency scaling.

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_REALTIME_COARSE
documentation: Identifier for system-wide realtime clock, updated only on ticks.

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_MONOTONIC_COARSE
documentation: Monotonic system-wide clock, updated only on ticks.

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_BOOTTIME
documentation: Monotonic system-wide clock that includes time spent in suspension.

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_REALTIME_ALARM
documentation: Like CLOCK_REALTIME but also wakes suspended system.

variables.name: quick2wire.timerfd.CLOCK_BOOTTIME_ALARM
documentation: Like CLOCK_BOOTTIME but also wakes suspended system.

classes.name: quick2wire.timerfd.Timer

methods.name: quick2wire.timerfd.Timer.__init__
documentation:
Creates a new Timer.

Arguments:
offset   -- the initial expiration time, measured in seconds from
            the call to start().
interval -- if non-zero, the interval for periodic timer expirations, 
            measured in seconds.
blocking -- if False calls to wait() do not block until the timer 
            expires but return 0 if the timer has not expired. 
            (default = True)
clock    -- the system clock used to measure time:
            CLOCK_REALTIME  -- system-wide realtime clock.
            CLOCK_MONOTONIC -- monotonic system-wide clock.

methods.name: quick2wire.timerfd.Timer.close
documentation: Closes the Timer and releases its file descriptor.

methods.name: quick2wire.timerfd.Timer.fileno
documentation: Returns the Timer's file descriptor.

methods.name: quick2wire.timerfd.Timer.offset
documentation: the initial expiration time, measured in seconds from the call to start().

methods.name: quick2wire.timerfd.Timer.interval
documentation:
The interval, specified in seconds, with which the timer will repeat.

If zero, the timer only fires once, when the offset expires.

methods.name: quick2wire.timerfd.Timer.start
documentation:
Starts the timer running.

Raises:
ValueError -- if offset and interval are both zero.

methods.name: quick2wire.timerfd.Timer.stop
documentation: Stops the timer running. Any scheduled timer events will not fire.

methods.name: quick2wire.timerfd.Timer.wait
documentation:
Receives timer events.

If the timer has already expired one or more times since its
settings were last modified or wait() was last called then
wait() returns the number of expirations that have occurred.

If no timer expirations have occurred, then the call either
blocks until the next timer expiration, or returns 0 if the
Timer is non-blocking (was created with the blocking parameter
set to False).

Raises:
OSError -- an OS error occurred reading the state of the timer.
A one-shot or repeating timer that can be added to a Selector.

classes.name: quick2wire.eventfd.Semaphore

methods.name: quick2wire.eventfd.Semaphore.__init__
documentation:
Creates a Semaphore with an initial count.

Arguments:
count -- the initial count.
blocking -- if False calls to wait() do not block if the Semaphore
            has a count of zero. (default = True)

methods.name: quick2wire.eventfd.Semaphore.close
documentation: Closes the Semaphore and releases its file descriptor.

methods.name: quick2wire.eventfd.Semaphore.fileno
documentation: Returns the Semaphore's file descriptor.

methods.name: quick2wire.eventfd.Semaphore.signal
documentation:
Signal the semaphore.

Signalling a semaphore increments its count by one and wakes a
blocked task that is waiting on the semaphore.

methods.name: quick2wire.eventfd.Semaphore.wait
documentation:
Receive a signal from the Semaphore, decrementing its count by one.

If the Semaphore is already has a count of zero, either wait
for a signal if the Semaphore is in blocking mode, or return
False immediately.

Returns:
True  -- the Semaphore received a signal.
False -- the Semaphore did not receive a signal and is in 
         non-blocking mode.
A Semaphore implemented with eventfd that can be added to a Selector.

modules.name: quick2wire.selector
documentation:
Event notification for I/O, timers, inter-thread and inter-process
communication.

classes.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.__init__
documentation:
Initialises a Selector.

Arguments:
size_hint -- A hint of the number of event sources that will
             be added to the Selector, or -1 for the default.
             Used to optimize internal data structures, it
             doesn't limit the maximum number of monitored
             event sources.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.fileno
documentation: Returns the Selector's file descriptor.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.add
documentation:
Adds an event source to the Selector.

Arguments:
source     -- the event source to add.  Must provide a fileno() 
              method that returns its file descriptor.
              
eventmask  -- the events that the Selector will report.  A
              bit-mask of:
              INPUT          -- there is input to be read from 
                                the source
              OUTPUT         -- output can be written to the source
              ERROR          -- an error has occurred on the source
              HANGUP         -- a remote hangup has occured on the 
                                source
              PRIORITY_INPUT -- urgent out-of-band data is waiting 
                                to be read from the source
              The default is INPUT|ERROR.
trigger    -- LEVEL -- the event source is level triggered (the 
              default),
              EDGE  -- the event source is edge triggered.
identifier -- A value to be stored in the `ready` property when an
              event has occurred on the source.  Default is the 
              source itself.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.remove
documentation:
Removes an event source from the Selector.

Arguments:
source -- the event source to remove.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.wait
documentation:
Wait for an event to occur on any of the sources that have been added to the Selector.

After wait returns, the `ready` property is set to the
identifier of a source that has an event that needs to be
responded to and the `events` property is set to a bit-set
indicating which events have occurred on the source.  The
`has_input`, `has_output`, `has_error`, `has_hangup` and
`has_priority_input` events provide convenient access to the
bits of the `event` bit-set.

If a timeout is specified and no events occur before the
timeout, the `ready` property is `None`.

Arguments: 
timeout -- maximum time to wait for an event. Specified in
           seconds (can be less than one). Default is no
           timeout: wait forever for an event.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.has_input
documentation: Returns whether the ready event source has input that can be read.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.has_output
documentation: Returns whether output can be written to the ready event source.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.has_error
documentation: Returns whetheran error has occurred on the ready event source.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.has_hangup
documentation: Returns whether a remote hangup has occured on the ready event source.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.has_priority_input
documentation: Returns whether urgent out-of-band data is waiting to be read from the ready event source.

methods.name: quick2wire.selector.Selector.close
documentation: Closes the Selector and releases its file descriptor.
Lets a thread wait for multiple events and handle them one at a time.

modules.name: quick2wire.gpio
documentation: A convenient API to access the GPIO pins of the Raspberry Pi.

classes.name: quick2wire.gpio.Pin

methods.name: quick2wire.gpio.Pin.__init__
documentation:
Creates a pin

Parameters:
user_pin_number -- the identity of the pin used to create the derived class.
soc_pin_number  -- the pin on the header to control, identified by the SoC pin number.
direction       -- (optional) the direction of the pin, either In or Out.
interrupt       -- (optional)
pull            -- (optional)

Raises:
IOError        -- could not export the pin (if direction is given)

methods.name: quick2wire.gpio.Pin.get
documentation:
The current value of the pin: 1 if the pin is high or 0 if the pin is low.

The value can only be set if the pin's direction is Out.

Raises: 
IOError -- could not read or write the pin's value.

methods.name: quick2wire.gpio.Pin.direction
documentation:
The direction of the pin: either In or Out.

The value of the pin can only be set if its direction is Out.

Raises:
IOError -- could not set the pin's direction.

methods.name: quick2wire.gpio.Pin.interrupt
documentation:
The interrupt property specifies what event (if any) will raise an interrupt.

One of: 
Rising  -- voltage changing from low to high
Falling -- voltage changing from high to low
Both    -- voltage changing in either direction
None    -- interrupts are not raised

Raises:
IOError -- could not read or set the pin's interrupt trigger

methods.name: quick2wire.gpio.Pin.fileno
documentation: Return the underlying file descriptor.  Useful for select, epoll, etc.

methods.name: quick2wire.gpio.Pin.closed
documentation: Returns if this pin is closed
Controls a GPIO pin.

modules.name: quick2wire.spi_ctypes
documentation: Warning: not part of the published Quick2Wire API.
User space versions of kernel symbols for SPI clocking modes,
matching <linux/spi/spi.h>
Ported to Python ctypes from <linux/spi/spidev.h>

functions.name: quick2wire.spi_ctypes.SPI_MSGSIZE
documentation: not all platforms use <asm-generic/ioctl.h> or _IOC_TYPECHECK() ...

variables.name: quick2wire.spi_ctypes.SPI_IOC_MAGIC
documentation: ord('k')

classes.name: quick2wire.spi_ctypes.spi_ioc_transfer
documentation: <linux/spi/spidev.h> struct spi_ioc_transfer
struct spi_ioc_transfer - describes a single SPI transfer
tx_buf:        Holds pointer to userspace buffer with transmit data, or null.
If no data is provided, zeroes are shifted out.
rx_buf:        Holds pointer to userspace buffer for receive data, or null.
len:           Length of tx and rx buffers, in bytes.
speed_hz:      Temporary override of the device's bitrate.
bits_per_word: Temporary override of the device's wordsize.
delay_usecs:   If nonzero, how long to delay after the last bit transfer
before optionally deselecting the device before the next transfer.
cs_change:     True to deselect device before starting the next transfer.
This structure is mapped directly to the kernel spi_transfer structure;
the fields have the same meanings, except of course that the pointers
are in a different address space (and may be of different sizes in some
cases, such as 32-bit i386 userspace over a 64-bit x86_64 kernel).
Zero-initialize the structure, including currently unused fields, to
accomodate potential future updates.
SPI_IOC_MESSAGE gives userspace the equivalent of kernel spi_sync().
Pass it an array of related transfers, they'll execute together.
Each transfer may be half duplex (either direction) or full duplex.
struct spi_ioc_transfer mesg[4];
status = ioctl(fd, SPI_IOC_MESSAGE(4), mesg);
So for example one transfer might send a nine bit command (right aligned
in a 16-bit word), the next could read a block of 8-bit data before
terminating that command by temporarily deselecting the chip; the next
could send a different nine bit command (re-selecting the chip), and the
last transfer might write some register values.

functions.name: quick2wire.spi.duplex
documentation:
An SPI transfer that writes the write_byte_sequence to the device and reads len(write_byte_sequence) bytes from the device.

The bytes to be written are passed to this function as a sequence.

functions.name: quick2wire.spi.duplex_bytes
documentation:
An SPI transfer that writes the write_bytes to the device and reads len(write_bytes) bytes from the device.

Each byte to be written is passed as an argument to this function.

functions.name: quick2wire.spi.reading
documentation: An SPI transfer that shifts out byte_count zero bytes and reads byte_counts bytes from the device.

functions.name: quick2wire.spi.writing
documentation:
An SPI transfer that writes one or more bytes of data and ignores any bytes read from the device.

The bytes are passed to this function as a sequence.

functions.name: quick2wire.spi.writing_bytes
documentation:
An SPI transfer that writes one or more bytes of data and ignores any bytes read from the device.

Each byte is passed as an argument to this function.

classes.name: quick2wire.spi.SPIDevice

methods.name: quick2wire.spi.SPIDevice.__init__
documentation:
Opens the SPI device.

Arguments:
chip_select -- the SPI chip select line to use. The Raspberry Pi
               only has two chip select lines, numbered 0 and 1.
bus         -- the number of the bus (default 0, the only SPI bus
               on the Raspberry Pi).

methods.name: quick2wire.spi.SPIDevice.transaction
documentation:
Perform an SPI I/O transaction.

Arguments:
*transfers -- SPI transfer requests created by one of the reading,
              writing, writing_bytes, duplex or duplex_bytes 
              functions.

Returns: a list of byte sequences, one for each read or duplex
         operation performed.

methods.name: quick2wire.spi.SPIDevice.close
documentation: Closes the file descriptor.

methods.name: quick2wire.spi.SPIDevice.clock_mode
documentation: Returns the current clock mode for the SPI bus

methods.name: quick2wire.spi.SPIDevice.clock_mode
documentation:
Changes the clock mode for this SPI bus

For example:
     #start clock low, sample trailing edge
     spi.clock_mode = SPI_MODE_1

methods.name: quick2wire.spi.SPIDevice.speed_hz
documentation: Returns the current speed in Hz for this SPI bus

methods.name: quick2wire.spi.SPIDevice.speed_hz
documentation: Changes the speed in Hz for this SPI bus

Communicates with a hardware device over an SPI bus.

Transactions are performed by passing one or more SPI I/O requests
to the transaction method of the SPIDevice.  SPI I/O requests are
created with the reading, writing, writing_bytes, duplex and
duplex_bytes functions defined in the quick2wire.spi module.

An SPIDevice acts as a context manager, allowing it to be used in
a with statement.  The SPIDevice's file descriptor is closed at
the end of the with statement and the instance cannot be used for
further I/O.

For example:

    from quick2wire.spi import SPIDevice, writing
    
    with SPIDevice(0) as spi0:
        spi0.transaction(
            writing(0x20, bytes([0x01, 0xFF])))

modules.name: py532lib.mifare
documentation:
This module provides convenient tools for the communication with
Mifare cards via the PN532.

Some knowledge of a Mifare card's layout and general access procedure
is needed to use this class effectively. Special care needs to be
taken when modifying trailer blocks because you may shut yourself
out of your card! Google "MF1S703x" for a good introduction to
Mifare cards.

A typical scenario would be:

card = Mifare()
card.SAMconfigure()
card.set_max_retries(MIFARE_SAFE_RETRIES)
uid = card.scan_field()
if uid:
    address = card.mifare_address(0,1)
    card.mifare_auth_a(address,MIFARE_FACTORY_KEY)
    data = card.mifare_read(address)
    card.in_deselect() # In case you want to authorize a different sector.

variables.name: py532lib.mifare.MIFARE_WAIT_FOR_ENTRY
documentation: MxRtyPassiveActivation value: wait until card enters field.

variables.name: py532lib.mifare.MIFARE_SAFE_RETRIES
documentation: This number of retries seems to detect most cards properlies.

classes.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.__init__
documentation: Set up and configure PN532.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.set_max_retries
documentation:
Configure the PN532 for the number of retries attempted
during the InListPassiveTarget operation (set to
MIFARE_SAFE_RETRIES for a safe one-time check, set to
MIFARE_WAIT_FOR_ENTRY so it waits until entry of a card).

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.scan_field
documentation:
Scans the PN532's field for a Mifare card using the
InListPassiveTarget operation.

Returns the card's UID (a bytearray) if a card was in the field
or False if no card was in the field. Only one card is
detected at a time (the PN532 can handle two but this is not
implemented here). False is never returned if the number of
retries (see set_max_retries()) is set to MIFARE_WAIT_FOR_ENTRY.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.in_data_exchange
documentation:
Sends a (Mifare) command to the currently active target.

The "data" parameter contains the command data as a bytearray.
Returns the data returned by the command (as a bytearray).
Raises an IOError if the command failed.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.in_deselect
documentation: Deselects the current target.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_address
documentation: Returns a one byte address for the given Mifare sector and block.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_sector_block
documentation: Returns a tuple (sector,block) for the given address.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_auth_a
documentation:
Authenticate the Mifare card with key A.

The "key_a" parameter is a bytearray that contains key A.
You may specify an address directly or use the mifare_address()
function to calculate it. Raises an IOError if authentication failed.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_auth_b
documentation:
Authenticate the Mifare card with key B.

The "key_a" parameter is a bytearray that contains key B.
You may specify an address directly or use the mifare_address()
function to calculate it. Raises an IOError if authentication failed.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_read
documentation: Read and return 16 bytes from the data block at the given address.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_write_standard
documentation:
Write 16 bytes to the data block on a Mifare Standard card
at the given address.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_write_ultralight
documentation:
Write 4 bytes to the data block on a Mifare Ultralight card
at the given address.

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_read_access
documentation:
Returns the access conditions for the block at the given address
in a three-tuple of booleans (C1,C2,C3).

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_write_access
documentation:
Changes the access conditions for the block at the given address
to the three booleans c1,c2,c3.

YOU SHOULD REALLY KNOW WHAT YOU'RE DOING HERE! With the wrong,
settings, you may shut yourself out of your card. The keys A
and B must also be provided because they cannot be read and
may therefore be overwritten by zeroes (as returned by a
regular read on the trailer sector).

methods.name: py532lib.mifare.Mifare.mifare_change_keys
documentation:
Changes the authorization keys A and B for the block at
the given address.

KEYS MAY NOT BE READABLE SO MAKE SURE YOU WRITE THEM DOWN!
If you forget a key (especially key A), you may not be able
to authenticate a block anymore and therefore not read it
or write to it. The factory preset for keys is always
b'ÿÿÿÿÿÿ' as defined in MIFARE_FACTORY_KEY.

This class allows for the communication with Mifare cards via
the PN532.

Compared to its superclass, this class provides a bit more
sophisticated tools such as reading the contents of a Mifare
card or writing to them, access restrictions, and key management.
